title: Polluted River Stretches (PRS) and Water Quality Criteria
focus: Definition of PRS, Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD) criteria, and geographical distribution of polluted stretches in India.
rationale: The criteria for PRS (BOD > 3 mg/L) and the geographical distribution (Maharashtra highest) are highly testable factual points for UPSC prelims.
sectionExcerpt: Two or more polluted locations on a river in a continuous sequence are considered as stretch and identified as PRS. Criteria: Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD) exceeds 3 milligrams/litre (mg/L). BOD is an indicator of water quality, measuring oxygen needed to break down organic matter. Categories: Five Priority Classes (I to V) based on maximum BOD observed... Total number of PRS has decreased from 351 (in 2018) to 296 (in 2025). Geographic Distribution: Maharashtra has highest number (54) of PRS.
evidenceBullets: PRS is identified when two or more polluted locations occur in a continuous sequence on a river.
The primary criterion for identifying a Polluted River Stretch is a Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD) exceeding 3 mg/L.
PRS are categorized into Five Priority Classes (I to V) based on the maximum BOD observed.
The total number of PRS in India has decreased from 351 in 2018 to 296 in 2025.
Maharashtra has the highest number of Polluted River Stretches (54) in India.

title: Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) and Environmental Compensation
focus: Statutory origins, functions, and powers of the CPCB and SPCBs, particularly regarding Environmental Compensation.
rationale: CPCB's statutory backing (Water Act vs Air Act vs EPA) and the Supreme Court ruling on Environmental Compensation are classic UPSC polity/environment overlaps.
sectionExcerpt: Genesis: Statutory body under Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974. Entrusted with powers and functions under Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981. Ministry: Ministry of Environment, Forest & Climate Change... Power to impose Environmental Compensation (EC): As per Supreme Court ruling, PCBs can impose or collect EC under Water Act, 1974 and Air Act, 1981 in case of environmental damage by erring entity or when damage is imminent, not for every violation.
evidenceBullets: CPCB is a statutory body established under the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974.
CPCB is also entrusted with powers and functions under the Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981.
CPCB provides technical services to the Environment Ministry under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986.
State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) are constituted under the same acts to implement environmental laws within state jurisdictions.
Per a Supreme Court ruling, PCBs can impose Environmental Compensation (EC) under the Water Act and Air Act for environmental damage, which is distinct from punitive action.

title: Framework and Programmes for River Rejuvenation
focus: Legal frameworks and major national programmes (Namami Gange, NMCG, NGRBA, NRCP) for river conservation.
rationale: Institutional structures like NMCG (registered society) and NGRBA (replacing National Ganga Council) are frequent targets for statement-based questions.
sectionExcerpt: Legal Framework: Water Act, 1974... Environment Protection Act, 1986... River Rejuvenation Programmes: Namami Gange Programme: Superseded Ganga Action Plan (GAP) launched in 1985 implemented by National Mission for Clean Ganga (NMCG) with River basin is the unit of planning and management. NMCG: Registered as a society in 2011 under Societies Registration Act, 1860, acts as the implementation arm of National Ganga River Basin Authority (NGRBA)... NGRBA: Constituted under EPA, 1986 replacing National Ganga Council (chaired by PM) in 2016... Centrally Sponsored Scheme of National River Conservation Plan (NRCP): In identified stretches of rivers, excluding in Ganga basin.
evidenceBullets: The Namami Gange Programme is implemented by the National Mission for Clean Ganga (NMCG), using the river basin as the unit of planning.
NMCG was registered as a society in 2011 under the Societies Registration Act, 1860.
NMCG acts as the implementation arm of the National Ganga River Basin Authority (NGRBA).
NGRBA was constituted under the Environment Protection Act, 1986, replacing the National Ganga Council in 2016.
The National River Conservation Plan (NRCP) is a Centrally Sponsored Scheme covering identified river stretches excluding the Ganga basin.

title: Desalination Technologies and Processes
focus: Mechanisms, merits, and demerits of Thermal and Membrane desalination technologies, and recent innovations.
rationale: Science & Tech application questions often test the differences between thermal (LTTD) and membrane (RO) technologies, as well as recent indigenous developments.
sectionExcerpt: Recently, IIT Bombay Scientists develop Lotus leaf-like Solar Evaporators for Salt-water Treatment, through a new hydrophobic Graphene-based material... Thermal Technology Concept: Heating of saline water and collecting condensed vapor... Example: Low Temperature Thermal Desalination (LTTD) plants in Kavaratti, Minicoy and Agatti Islands in Lakshadweep. Membrane Technology Concept: Feedwater is pumped through semi-permeable membranes to filter out dissolved solids. Usage: Brackish water desalination... Example: Nemmeli Seawater Desalination Plant, Tamil Nadu.
evidenceBullets: IIT Bombay scientists developed Lotus leaf-like Solar Evaporators using a hydrophobic Graphene-based material for water desalination.
Thermal desalination involves heating saline water and collecting condensed vapor, commonly used for seawater desalination.
Low Temperature Thermal Desalination (LTTD) plants are operational in Lakshadweep islands like Kavaratti, Minicoy, and Agatti.
Membrane technology uses semi-permeable membranes to filter dissolved solids, primarily for brackish water desalination.
Membrane technologies are susceptible to fouling and may not fully remove Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) or pathogens.

title: Revised Classification of Industries by CPCB
focus: The introduction of a new color-coded category for industrial classification by the CPCB.
rationale: The addition of the Blue category to the existing Red, Orange, Green, and White classifications is a specific, testable update.
sectionExcerpt: Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) directed State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) to adopt revised classification of industries. About Revised Classification: Sectors classified into Red, Orange, Green, White and new Blue category.
evidenceBullets: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has directed SPCBs to adopt a revised classification of industries.
The revised classification introduces a new Blue category alongside the existing Red, Orange, Green, and White categories.
